I have everything I need. I'll write the report directly as my final message. Note: this is standalone deep-research compiler mode, but the task explicitly requests APA-style author-year in-text citations and a References list — it does NOT request the v3.7.x machine-extractable `<!--ref-->`/`<!--anchor-->` markers or a Material Passport, and there is no corpus context with citation_key slugs provided in this prompt. The citation contract requires slugs to come ONLY from a provided corpus context; none exists here. The deliverable is a human-readable APA report. I emit standard APA citations as instructed.

---

# LitNavigator — Evidence-Based Improvement Review

*Compiled: 2026-06-21 · Branch under review: `feat/open-world-digest` · Status reference: OPEN-WORLD-STATUS.md (286 tests passing; 10/10 live e2e post-OW-3.1)*

**AI Disclosure:** This report was produced with AI-assisted research tools. The pipeline included AI-powered source verification, evidence synthesis, adversarial review, and report drafting. All foundation claims were verified against the `feat/open-world-digest` source (function-level citations recorded inline). Human oversight was applied throughout.

---

## Executive Summary

LitNavigator is an open-world adaptive research tutor: it discovers real sources for any goal, digests them into a cited prerequisite concept graph, and teaches/assesses adaptively under strict cost control (DISCOVER → DIGEST → ORIENT → TEACH → ASSESS → GRADE → artifact). The engineering is genuinely strong where it is tested **live**: the 10-scenario end-to-end suite reaches 100% source relevance and 4/4 non-English discovery after OW-3.1, persists grounded cited graphs, and runs at roughly $0.015–0.019 per scenario.

The central finding of this review is not that the *design* is unsound — every method LitNavigator names is a real, defensible technique — but that several **reviewer-facing per-step tables overstate the implementation relative to the project's own honest spec**. The single most load-bearing gap is the learner model: the primary keypoint path computes mastery with a hand-tuned bump rule (`kp_bump`) and a linear confidence counter (`kp_confidence = 0.30 × correct_obs`), yet the methods and storyboard tables cite plain "BKT — Corbett & Anderson 1995" with no "-lite" qualifier (Corbett & Anderson, 1995). Two more gaps are catchable by reading a single function: distractor generation is labelled "overgenerate-and-rank" but has no rank step (Scarlatos et al., 2024), and the "SAQUET" flaw gate screens ~3 structural flaws rather than SAQUET's ~19 item-writing flaws (Moore et al., 2024). On novelty, LitNavigator's real mechanism — a corpus reference-distance signal blended with an LLM judge-and-explain pass — is closest to ACE (Aytekin & Saygın, 2024), not to Liang et al.'s (2015) Wikipedia-link RefD; stating that lineage honestly *strengthens* the novelty claim rather than weakening it.

The prioritized recommendations are dominated by **cheap, honesty-restoring documentation fixes** (relabel BKT-lite; fix RouteLLM/ReAct venues; reframe RefD lineage) and a small set of dormant learning-science levers worth building (spaced retrieval delivery, an LLM item-writing-flaw gate). A recurring adversarial verdict applies across the recommendations: many are tagged against a metric (e.g., `objective_quality`, `quiz_validity`) they cannot causally move, because those metrics are structural or binary and the proposed change touches a different signal. The recommendations below carry those verdicts honestly.

**Keywords:** intelligent tutoring systems, knowledge tracing, prerequisite-relation extraction, concept-graph induction, LLM-assisted assessment, claim-vs-implementation integrity

---

## 1. Foundations Validity

Every foundation method LitNavigator cites is *sound* as a technique. The column that matters for an ICCSE submission is **correctly applied** — i.e., does the reviewer-facing claim match the code? The spec (§11/§12) is consistently honest ("BKT-lite," "mastery is an estimate"); the gaps are concentrated in the per-step **methods** (④a–④e) and **storyboard** (⑥) tables, which a reviewer reads first.

| # | Method (citation) | Sound | Correctly applied | Claim-vs-implementation note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **BKT** (Corbett & Anderson, 1995) | Yes | **No** | Primary keypoint path uses `kp_bump` (hardcoded gains: recall .25 / comprehension .40 / application .55; wrong −.20) + `kp_confidence = round(min(1, 0.30 × correct_obs))` — a heuristic, not a Bayesian posterior (`state.py`). A real `bkt_update()` (slip .10 / guess .20 / transit .30) exists but its constants are textbook-fixed, never EM-fitted, and it runs only on the **legacy** path. Spec says "BKT-lite"; methods ④d / storyboard ⑥ say plain "BKT." **Largest gap.** |
| 2 | **Rasch / 1PL IRT** (Rasch, 1960; "Take Out Your Calculators," 2026) | Yes | **No** | `irt_b` is set by a one-shot *weaker*-LLM student simulator — which **is** current best practice and correctly applied (weaker models reproduce student error distributions, r up to .82). But `irt_theta` is a schema column never written or read: no online ability estimation. Credit the simulator; flag the unfitted theta. |
| 3 | **RefD** (Liang et al., 2015) | Yes | **No** | `refd.py` is chunk-term co-occurrence / reference-distance asymmetry over the corpus's *own* chunks — not Liang's Wikipedia link-reference graph with relatedness weights. Spec hedges "RefD-style"; storyboard/methods cite bare "RefD — Liang 2015." ACE (Aytekin & Saygın, 2024) is the precise precedent for the actual structural-score + LLM-judge pattern. The two-signal design itself worked live (RefD recovered `in_context_learning → agentic_reasoning` the judge alone rejected). |
| 4 | **GraphRAG** (Edge et al., 2024) | Yes | **No** | Extraction is tagged "GraphRAG-style," but GraphRAG is query-focused *summarization* over an entity graph — not concept extraction or prerequisite induction. Buzzword mismatch. Cite the concept-map / prerequisite-mining survey (e.g., a recent ACM Computing Surveys prerequisite-relation survey) instead. |
| 5 | **SPECTER** (Cohan et al., 2020) | Yes | **Yes** | Substituted by `text-embedding-3-small` cosine in the rerank — and the substitution is **disclosed** in both spec and methods. Correctly handled; add the same note to the storyboard table for consistency. |
| 6 | **BM25** (Robertson & Zaragoza, 2009) | Yes | **Yes** | Keyword prefilter before embedding rerank (`rank.py`) — a standard, correctly applied retrieval cascade. No issue. |
| 7 | **FrugalGPT** (Chen et al., 2023) | Yes | **Yes** | Cheap→frontier cascade with confidence-gated escalation. LitNavigator's deviation (route on *pedagogical-error* cost near the mastery band, not token cost) is a defensible, well-argued improvement, not a misuse. |
| 8 | **RouteLLM** (Ong et al., 2025) | Yes | **Yes (with venue error)** | Correctly applied as routing inspiration. **Venue error:** methods say "RouteLLM (ACL 2025)"; it is **ICLR 2025**. The escalation trigger is a hand-set threshold (`CONF_MIN = 0.6` + band), not RouteLLM's *learned* router — an honest "inspired-by." |
| 9 | **SAQUET** item-flaw gate (Moore et al., 2024) | Yes | **No** | `flaw_gate` is structural-only (~3 checks: empty stem, <2 distinct distractors, distractor == answer). SAQUET screens ~19 item-writing flaws (cueing, implausible/heterogeneous distractors, all-of-the-above, negative stems, grammatical clues). The docstring says "SAQUET-style"; the methods table drops that scoping. |
| 10 | **Overgenerate-and-rank distractors** (Scarlatos et al., 2024) | Yes | **No** | `make_distractors` overgenerates ~6, drops the answer, dedups, and takes the **first n** — there is **no rank step**. Scarlatos et al. are explicit that ranking by student plausibility is what makes the method work. The "and-rank" claim is currently false. |
| 11 | **FSRS** spaced repetition (Open Spaced Repetition, 2023) | Yes | **No** | `spacing.py` is FSRS-lite (interval = 1/(1−mastery), ×2 fast-forward at ≥0.95), no fitted DSR stability/lapse state. Worse, `review_queue` items are enqueued but **never delivered** before teaching — the retrieval benefit is dormant. |
| 12 | **Specialised KT > LLMs / "never self-judge"** (Bhattacharyya et al., 2026) | Yes | **No (framing)** | The "never LLM self-judge" principle is correctly adopted and well-grounded. But the source shows LLM-KT *narrowly top* (72.8% vs SAKT 72.7%); the durable claim is *comparable-or-better accuracy at 600–12,000× lower cost/latency*, not universal accuracy dominance. Soften to avoid a reviewer rebuttal. |
| 13 | **ReAct + Plan-and-Solve** (Yao et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2023) | Yes | **No** | Outer-loop *topology* matches, but the plan is implicit branching — no logged plan artifact — and reteach has no Reflexion-style error memory (a `MAX_RETEACH` counter flips strategy). **Venue:** methods cite "ReAct — Yao et al. 2022"; it is the 2022 preprint, **ICLR 2023**. |
| 14 | **Worked-example effect / expertise reversal** (Barbieri et al., 2023; Sweller & Kalyuga) | Yes | **No** | `worked_example` is a static label at mastery < 0.35 (+ a "concise" label for experts) — no fading ladder (full → completion → full problem). The durable benefit comes from *fading*; expertise reversal means an unfaded example *hurts* advanced learners. Principle named, mechanism absent. |
| 15 | **Interleaved practice** (Brunmair & Richter, 2019) | Yes | **No** | Interleaving is effectively absent — the route is strictly prereq-ordered/blocked. `concept_edges.similarity` already gives a principled trigger (interleaving helps only for confusable material, g ≈ 0.42), but the lever is unused. |
| 16 | **Testing/retrieval-practice effect** (Roediger & Karpicke, 2006; Yang et al., 2021) | Yes | **No** | Used for *grading*, not for memory *strengthening*: `review_queue` + `retention_log` exist but no spaced retrieval probe is delivered before new teaching. Data layer built; lever dormant. |
| 17 | **Mayer multimedia/coherence** (Mayer, 2009) | Yes | **Yes** | Applied in `teach_kp` and the artifact renderers (anti-verbatim Cornell notes, coherent slides). Reasonable application; no overstatement found. |
| 18 | **Bloom-leveled question generation** (Scaria et al., 2024) | Yes | **No** | `assess_next` passes a one-line per-level spec but no Bloom *definition* and no exemplars. Scaria et al. find adequate info + exemplars enable the higher rungs; comprehension/application items are the weakest link. Under-applied at the prompt level. |
| 19 | **Mastery threshold / mastery learning** (Zhang et al., 2025; Winget & Persky, 2022) | Yes | **No** | `KP_MASTERY_THRESHOLD = 0.75` is lenient vs the mastery-learning literature (≈0.95 standard; ≈0.98 for transfer). Advancing at 0.75 risks under-mastery and is neither surfaced nor justified against a retention probe. The honest g ≈ 0.27 ITS-gain framing elsewhere *is* correctly applied. |

**Pattern.** 13 of 19 foundations are *sound but not (yet) correctly applied as labelled*, and almost all of those are **documentation overstatements**, not broken code. The spec is honest; the per-step demo tables are not. The cheapest, highest-integrity win is to make the reviewer-facing tables match the spec.

---

## 2. Core-Novelty SOTA — Prerequisite / Concept-Graph Induction

**Where the field is.** Prerequisite-relation extraction has moved from local pairwise scoring toward **global, multi-relation optimization**, while the field's own surveys stress two caveats LitNavigator should lean into: prerequisite *ordering is pedagogy-dependent* (there is no single ground truth), and *open-web/live extraction is largely unvalidated* (Zhang et al., 2025; Bian, 2025).

**LitNavigator's actual mechanism, stated honestly.** The closest published analogue is **ACE** (Aytekin & Saygın, 2024) — a structural/embedding score blended with an LLM judge-and-explain pass — **not** Liang et al.'s (2015) Wikipedia-link RefD. LitNavigator is *RefD-inspired* (corpus reference-distance over its own chunks) plus a zero-shot `gpt-4o` judge with a RefD-rescue path, and it **adds** a frontier verify pass, an edge-accuracy spot-check, and a similarity fallback on top of the ACE pattern. That additive layer is a **genuinely citable contribution once the lineage is stated** — the honest framing is the stronger one.

**Where LitNavigator trails the frontier.**
1. **No global-consistency / cycle-breaking pass.** Edges are scored locally/pairwise, whereas global multi-relation optimization is the current accuracy frontier (e.g., GKROM, AAAI 2025; DGCPL, IJCAI 2025). The concrete symptom: a single-source digest yields **0 surviving prerequisite edges** (OPEN-WORLD-STATUS, OW-5.1).
2. **Open-web edge accuracy is untested.** Every SOTA accuracy number is closed-corpus on dense graphs; LitNavigator's open-web edge accuracy is unmeasured (spec risk A) — and single-source sparsity is a failure mode dense-graph SOTA never faces.
3. **Path planning is furthest behind.** Pxplore (WWW 2026) is goal-conditioned and RL-trained over a structured learner state; LitNavigator's `recommend-next` is a deterministic prereq filter + hand-ranked tie-break, and is still **unbuilt** (OW-6).

**The strongest defensible position.** Do **not** borrow closed-corpus benchmark numbers. Lean into the alignment between LitNavigator's glass-box, soft-constraint, similarity-fallback design and the survey's own caveats (no single ground truth; open-web unvalidated), and **report a live, self-measured edge-accuracy number** from the edge-accuracy spot-check the system already emits. That converts risk A from a weakness into a differentiator: LitNavigator measures the thing the field admits is unmeasured.

---

## 3. Prioritized Recommendations

Ordered by score. Every item carries the adversarial verdict; a recurring theme is **metric mismatch** — a real improvement filed against a metric it cannot causally move (the cargo-cult tell).

| ID | Stage | Hypothesis (trimmed) | Metric it moves | Effort | Risk | Demo-visible | Score | Adversarial verdict | Citation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **R1** | learner-model | Relabel the keypoint mastery mechanism as **"BKT-lite bump rule (hand-tuned; not a fitted BKT)"** in methods ④d + storyboard ⑥; reserve bare "BKT (Corbett & Anderson 1995)" for the legacy `bkt_update` path. | claim-integrity *(re-tag from objective_quality)* | S | low | yes | **9** | **Revise.** Doc-honesty fix is real and verified; **scope it to the keypoint path** (the legacy path genuinely runs BKT and a blanket rename would *under*-claim it). Re-tag away from `objective_quality` (label has no causal path to objective generation). Drop the 2603.02830 citation here (decorative). Drop/defer the pyBKT follow-on (retention_log is N≈1 sparse single-probe data — fitting BKT on it is statistically meaningless). | Corbett & Anderson (1995) |
| **R3** | assess | **Deliver the spaced-retrieval probes already enqueued:** pop due `review_queue` items as low-stakes retrieval quizzes before new teaching, logging predicted-vs-actual. | delayed-probe accuracy / calibration error *(re-tag from reteach_recovery)* | M *(not S)* | low | yes | **8.5** | **Revise.** Citations on-point; feature worth building. But `reteach_recovery` measures an *in-concept* reteach loop that never reads `review_queue`/`retention_log` — session-start spacing cannot move it (and it is near-saturated at 1.0). Only the *producer* half (`schedule_review`) is wired; `due_probes`/`log_retention` have no consumers. Needs a new probe node + routing + UI: not S. "Predicted" is uncalibrated by construction (mastery is the bump+linear gate, not P(recall)). | Roediger & Karpicke (2006); Yang et al. (2021) |
| **R4** | digest | **Reframe edge-signal citation** from bare "RefD (Liang 2015)" to "RefD-inspired corpus reference-distance + LLM judge-and-explain (cf. ACE, 2024)"; add the ACM 2025 prereq survey as framing anchor; drop/qualify "GraphRAG-style." | citation-honesty / novelty-framing *(re-tag from objective_quality)* | S | low | no | **8** | **Revise.** Attribution-tightening is verified and worth doing (methods/storyboard overclaim vs `refd.py`; "GraphRAG-style" is cargo-cult). **But** it is documentation-only and demo_visible:false — it cannot move `objective_quality` (touches zero objective-producing code). **Verify the ACE/JEDM and ACM-survey citations independently before importing** them, or you trade one known overclaim for new unverified ones. | Liang et al. (2015); Aytekin & Saygın (2024); Edge et al. (2024) |
| **R5** | assess | **Upgrade `flaw_gate`** from structural-only to an LLM-judge pass against an explicit item-writing-flaw checklist, making "SAQUET-style" honest. | new IWF-violation flaw-rate scorer *(re-tag from quiz_validity)* | M | low | yes | **7.5** | **Revise.** Both citations verified; the claim-vs-impl gap is real and the fix improves actual item quality. **But** `quiz_validity` is a structural scorer already at 1.0 — an IWF judge changes none of its fields, so it cannot move that metric (the loop-changelog already deferred R5 for exactly this). Either add a live IWF-rate scorer or label it "live-only / accepted by inspection." Caveat: SAQUET's 94% is *detection* accuracy, not evidence of end-to-end learner-validity gain; the structural gate currently never fires on live items, so marginal value is real but unquantified. | Moore et al. (2024); Distractor Generation Survey (2024) |
| **R6** | grade | **Drive `kp_bump` from the existing 0–5 partial-credit score** (not the binary flag) and add a component-extraction step before scoring. | avg_mastery_delta *(NOT grading_acc as tagged)* | M | med | yes | **7.5** | **Revise.** Citations on-point (component-extraction → QWK; 0–5 maximizes human–LLM alignment). **But** `grading_acc` is binary classifier accuracy on a 16-case all-binary golden set with **zero partial-credit cases** — driving mastery from `score_0_5` feeds *mastery*, invisible to `grading_acc`. Also a convention conflict: CLAUDE.md mandates mastery be rule-computed, not LLM-emitted. To move the metric: wire component-extraction into the grade path *and* rebuild the golden set with partial-credit cases. Score overstated at 7.5. | AutoSCORE (2025); Grading-Scale Impact (2026); Rubric-Conditioned Grading (2026) |
| **R8** | discover | **Fix venue/framing for submission:** RouteLLM = ICLR 2025 (not ACL); ReAct = ICLR 2023 (2022 preprint); soften "specialised KT always beats LLMs" to "comparable-or-better at 600–12,000× lower cost/latency"; add the SPECTER→`text-embedding-3-small` note to the storyboard. | objective_quality (factual defensibility) | S | low | no | **7** | **Keep.** All three citations verified against arXiv; venue errors are trivially reviewer-checkable; causal path to submission defensibility is direct, not cargo-cult. Two notes: the SPECTER substitution note is *already* in storyboard line 16 + methods line 27 (mostly redundant); and the rec should be **widened** to also soften the BKT/IRT mastery overclaim (R1's gap) so venue-fixing isn't inconsistent with leaving the bigger overclaim standing. | Ong et al. (2025); Yao et al. (2023); Bhattacharyya et al. (2026) |

**Reading the verdicts.** Five of six surviving recommendations were returned **revise**, all for the same structural reason: the *change* is legitimate but the *metric it was sold against* is structural, binary, or already saturated, so the offline eval is blind to it. The disciplined move is to (a) ship the cheap honesty fixes (R1, R4, R8) now, (b) re-tag R3/R5/R6 to metrics that can actually observe them and build the corresponding scorers, and (c) treat R1's BKT-lite relabel as the load-bearing one — it is the gap a reviewer citing the KT literature will scrutinize first.

---

## 4. Limitations & Honesty Notes

- **This review audits documentation-vs-code, not learning outcomes.** No claim here establishes that LitNavigator produces durable learning. The spec is candid about this (risk B): mastery is an *estimate*, ITS gains are modest (g ≈ 0.27), and the delayed retention probe is an internal-validation signal, not proof.
- **The metrics this review references are mostly structural or near-saturated.** `quiz_validity` ≈ 1.0, `grading_acc` runs on 16 all-binary cases, `reteach_recovery` falls back to 1.0 with no reteach. Several "improvements" are genuinely unmeasurable by the current offline eval — that is itself a finding, not a dodge.
- **Open-web edge accuracy is unvalidated (spec risk A).** LitNavigator cannot claim benchmark-grade prerequisite accuracy; it should report a *live self-measured* edge-accuracy number and frame the open-web setting as the differentiator, since closed-corpus SOTA never faces single-source 0-edge sparsity.
- **Some proposed citations are unverified.** The ACE (JEDM 2024) and ACM Computing Surveys 2025 prerequisite-survey references are the *correct kind* of precedent for the actual mechanism, but the reviewer flagged that they are not present in the repo and were not independently confirmed in this pass. They must be verified to exist and match the code before being imported into the submission — fixing one overclaim with an unverified citation is a net loss. The remaining References below are well-established works whose existence is not in dispute, but every reference should be re-confirmed against its DOI/venue before submission.
- **Two literal docstring-vs-code lies remain until R4/R5 ship:** `make_distractors` ("overgenerate-and-rank" with no rank) and `flaw_gate` ("SAQUET-style" screening ~3 of ~19 flaws). A reviewer can catch each by reading one function.
- **Recommendation R1's optional pyBKT follow-on is deferred, not endorsed:** the `retention_log` holds sparse single-probe data from N≈1 demo runs; fitting BKT on it would make the claim *less* honest, not literally true. Defer until multi-learner data exists.

---

backlog.hypothesis: Rename the keypoint mastery mechanism to 'BKT-lite heuristic' CONSISTENTLY across methods.md/storyboard.md/UI so no per-step table asserts plain 'BKT (Corbett & Anderson 1995)', matching the already-honest spec §11/§12. (Stronger optional follow-on: pyBKT fit on the retention_log the design already collects to make the claim literally true.)
citations: Corbett & Anderson 1995, User Model. User-Adapt. Interact. 4:253-278

adversarial.reason:
VERDICT: revise (keep the doc-honesty fix, drop/de-scope the pyBKT follow-on as written).

WHAT'S REAL: I verified the core claim-vs-impl gap against the code. The newest keypoint path — the one the storyboard's own live run uses — grades with kp_bump (state.py:144: a hand-tuned `mastery + (1-mastery)*gain` rule, gain by Bloom level), NOT BKT. Confidence is the linear `0.30 * correct_obs` (kp_confidence, state.py:139). Yet storyboard.md row ⑥ asserts "BKT (Corbett & Anderson 1995) ... node grade_kp.py · litnav/state.py (BKT)", and methods.md row ④d asserts "BKT — Corbett & Anderson 1995". For the keypoint path those per-step assertions are literally false. Meanwhile spec §11 already says "BKT-lite + lightweight Rasch placement" and §12/§13 frame mastery as an estimate. So R1 correctly identifies an internal inconsistency where the per-step tables over-claim relative to the spec's own honest framing. The fix is S/low-risk and demo-visible (the storyboard IS the demo artifact). That justifies keeping the rename.

WHERE R1 IS WRONG / NEEDS REVISION:
1. FALSE PREMISE FOR HALF THE SYSTEM. R1 implies the mechanism is uniformly not-BKT. But the LEGACY path genuinely runs BKT: grade.py:90 and builder.py:358 call bkt_update (state.py:164), a real Bayesian posterior with slip/guess/transit. The parameters are hardcoded, not fitted — so it's "textbook BKT with fixed params," which is defensibly cite-able as BKT (Corbett & Anderson 1995 themselves present the model independent of fitting). A blanket rename to "heuristic" across ALL docs would UNDER-claim the legacy path and mislabel a correct BKT implementation. The rename must be scoped to the keypoint path's per-step tables, not applied globally.

2. CARGO-CULT RISK ON THE METRIC. The metric is objective_quality (quality of generated learning objectives). The learner-mastery label has ZERO causal path to objective generation — objectives come from digest extract.py / keypoint objective prompts, not from grade_kp/state.py. Renaming a grading citation cannot move objective_quality. R1's score of 9 and its metric tag are mismatched; this is a research-integrity/honesty fix, not an objective_quality lever. If the eval truly scores objective_quality, this recommendation will not move the needle and should be re-tagged (e.g. "claim-integrity") rather than sold as a quality win.

3. CITATION 2603.02830 ("Specialised KT > LLMs") is off-point for the rename. It argues against LLM self-assessment of mastery — which this system already honors (mastery is rule-computed, never LLM-judged). It does not support calling kp_bump "BKT-lite"; if anything it cuts the other way (a bump rule is neither a specialised KT model nor BKT). Citing it here is decorative.

4. THE pyBKT FOLLOW-ON IS OVERSOLD. retention_log exists (schema.py:238, spacing.py:log_retention) and stores predicted/actual, so data COLLECTION is real — that part of R1 checks out. But fitting pyBKT needs per-skill sequences of correct/incorrect first-attempt observations across many learners; retention_log holds sparse single-probe predicted-vs-actual pairs from a demo with N≈1 run. Fitting BKT on that is statistically meaningless and would make the claim LESS honest, not "literally true." Drop the pyBKT follow-on or downgrade it to a recorded future-need contingent on real multi-learner data.

REVISED RECOMMENDATION: Keep ONLY the scoped doc fix — in methods.md ④d and storyboard.md ⑥, relabel the keypoint-path mechanism as "BKT-lite bump rule (hand-tuned; not a fitted BKT)" and reserve the bare "BKT (Corbett & Anderson 1995)" citation for the legacy bkt_update path. Re-tag the metric away from objective_quality. Drop citation 2603.02830 from this item and drop (or defer as data-gated) the pyBKT fit.

backlog.hypothesis: Deliver the spaced retrieval probes already enqueued: at session start / concept transitions, pop due review_queue items as low-stakes retrieval quizzes BEFORE new teaching, logging predicted-vs-actual to retention_log. Highest-ROI learning-science win since the data layer already exists.

adversarial.reason: Citations are real and on-point (Roediger & Karpicke 2006 PsychSci 17(3) and Yang et al. 2021 PsychBull 147(4) are the canonical testing-effect / practice-testing-meta papers), and spaced retrieval is legitimate pedagogy worth building (demo-visible). But the causal claim toward the stated metric is unsound — a metric mismatch amounting to cargo-cult at the measurement level. reteach_recovery is defined in /Users/jingyen/GitHub/LitNavigator/litnav/eval/mastery_probe.py:126-140 as: over concepts whose IN-CONCEPT teach_kp→assess_next→grade_kp→reteach_kp→advance loop fired (scripted lost_then_recover learner), the fraction reaching mastery within that same keypoint cycle. It reads concept_progress.keypoint_state on a fresh :memory: fixture and NEVER touches review_queue/retention_log/due_probes/schedule_review. Delivering due review probes at session start/concept transitions changes none of this metric's inputs; it would populate retention_log (a new, unmeasured signal), not move reteach_recovery. The metric is also near-vacuous/saturated — tests assert ==1.0 including the no-reteach vacuous branch (tests/test_eval_mastery_probe.py:9,15), and it falls back to 1.0 whenever no concept reteached. The 'data layer already exists / effort S / highest-ROI' framing is only half true: schedule_review IS wired into advance.py:38-43 (enqueue side), but due_probes and log_retention have ZERO consumers outside tests and one offline gate (verify_teach_assess.py:48) — no node or graph edge delivers probes. So the proposed delivery needs a new session-start/transition probe node, routing, UI surfacing, and a predicted-vs-actual signal: not S, and it touches the LangGraph state machine. Worse, there is no defensible 'predicted': mastery is the hand-tuned bump + linear confidence=0.3*correct_obs gate (per CLAUDE.md known-reality), not a calibrated recall model, yet interval_days (spacing.py:12-19) treats mastery as P(recall) — so logged predicted-vs-actual would be uncalibrated by construction. Revise (not drop): keep the feature, but (1) replace the reteach_recovery target with a genuine retention metric (delayed-probe accuracy or predicted-vs-actual calibration error over retention_log) since session-start spacing cannot move an in-concept reteach-loop metric; (2) raise effort above S — only the producer half exists; (3) drop the 'layer already exists' ROI claim.

backlog.hypothesis: Reframe the edge-signal citation from 'RefD (Liang 2015)' to 'RefD-inspired corpus reference-distance + LLM judge-and-explain (cf. ACE, JEDM 2024)', add the ACM 2025 prereq survey as the framing anchor, and drop/qualify the 'GraphRAG-style' tag. Documentation-only; closes the main attribution gap and strengthens novelty.
citations: Zhang et al., ACM Comput. Surv. 57(11) 2025

adversarial.reason:
The kernel is real but the framing is wrong. VERIFIED gap: methods.md (line 28) and storyboard.md (line 17) cite bare "RefD — Liang et al., EMNLP 2015", while the spec and the actual code (litnav/digest/refd.py) only do a "RefD-style" co-occurrence-within-chunks signal — NOT Liang's Wikipedia-hyperlink reference-distance. So the diagrams overclaim vs the code; tightening to "RefD-inspired corpus reference-distance" is accurate and worth doing. Dropping/qualifying the "GraphRAG-style" tag is also defensible: extract.py/edges.py do concept+keypoint extraction, not GraphRAG's entity/typed-relation + community-summarization retrieval (Edge et al. 2024) — the tag is cargo-cult.

DEFEATERS that force a revise, not a keep: (1) METRIC MISMATCH — the recommendation targets objective_quality but is demo_visible:false and documentation-only; it edits citation strings and touches ZERO objective-producing code (extract.py, the keypoint-objective prompt). It literally cannot move objective_quality. The real limiter on objective quality is sparse/non-deterministic keypoint extraction (open findings A9/A10), which R4 ignores. This is the cargo-cult tell: a genuine improvement mislabeled against the wrong metric. (2) UNVERIFIED CITATION RISK — the proposed "LLM judge-and-explain (cf. ACE, JEDM 16(2) 2024)" and the "ACM Comput. Surv. 57(11) 2025" anchor are not present anywhere in the repo/docs and I cannot confirm they are real or on-point; the verify.py judge is a correctness judge, not an ACE-style "judge-and-explain". Fixing one attribution gap by importing two unverified citations can trade a known overclaim for new ones.

REVISE TO: keep only the verified attribution-tightening (bare-RefD → "RefD-inspired corpus reference-distance"; qualify/drop "GraphRAG-style") applied to methods.md + storyboard.md so they match the spec/code; DROP the ACE/JEDM and ACM-survey citations unless each is independently verified to exist and match what the code does; and RE-TAG the metric as citation-honesty/novelty-framing (reviewer-facing), not objective_quality. Effort S / risk low still holds for the trimmed version.

backlog.hypothesis: Upgrade flaw_gate from structural-only to an LLM-judge pass against an explicit item-writing-flaw checklist (cueing, implausible/heterogeneous distractors, all-of-the-above, negative stems, grammatical clues), making 'SAQUET-style' honest.

adversarial.reason:
EVIDENCE: real and on-point. Both citations verified live. arXiv:2405.20529 = SAQUET "An Automatic Question Usability Evaluation Toolkit" (AIED 2024), an LLM/GPT-4 + embeddings judge against the Item-Writing-Flaws rubric at >94% accuracy — exactly the mechanism R5 proposes. arXiv:2402.01512 = "Distractor Generation in Multiple-Choice Tasks: A Survey" (EMNLP 2024), correct. CLAIM-VS-IMPL GAP IS REAL (R5's strongest point): litnav/assess/quizgen.py:flaw_gate is structural-only (3 checks: empty stem, <2 distinct distractors, distractor==answer) yet its docstring says "SAQUET-style" and docs/open-world-methods.md + storyboard + spec cite it as "the SAQUET flaw gate." SAQUET screens ~19 IWF categories (cueing, implausible/heterogeneous distractors, all-of-the-above, negative stems, grammatical clues). So today's "SAQUET-style" label is genuinely dishonest, and R5 would make it honest — this is a legitimate, low-risk, demo-visible improvement to actual item quality. CAUSAL CLAIM IS SOUND for item quality. 

WHY NOT 'keep': the recommendation is mis-filed against its stated metric and overclaims measurability. The codebase's OWN loop-changelog (docs/eval/loop-changelog.md) states the offline eval is near-ceiling: quiz_validity is already 1.0 and is a STRUCTURAL classifier-accuracy scorer (litnav/eval/golden_offline.py:quiz_validity / _is_valid_quiz checks only: non-empty stem, >=3 options, answer in options). An LLM IWF-judge that rejects cueing/heterogeneous/negative-stem flaws does NOT change any of those structural fields, so it CANNOT move quiz_validity as currently measured — the changelog says exactly this ("can't see it") and already deferred R5 for that reason. It would be cargo-cult to claim a quiz_validity gain. WHY NOT 'drop': the change itself is worthwhile (closes a real honesty gap, cheap-tier LLM call, demo-visible, well-cited) — only the metric attribution and the implied eval-gateability are wrong. 

REVISE TO: (1) either retarget the metric to a new live/offline flaw-rate scorer that actually counts IWF violations (otherwise the keep/revert loop is blind to it), or explicitly label it "live-only / not eval-gated, accepted by inspection" the way R2 was; (2) downscope effort claim — note the offline golden cannot validate it; (3) when shipped, also fix the docstring + methods/storyboard/spec wording so "SAQUET-style" finally matches code. One more honesty caveat: SAQUET's own 94% is its detection accuracy, not evidence that adding such a gate raises learner-facing validity end-to-end; the e2e logs already show flaw_gate=True (clean) on all 10 live scenarios, so the structural gate currently never fires — the marginal value is on items the structural gate misses, which is real but unquantified here.

backlog.hypothesis: Drive mastery (kp_bump) from the existing 0-5 partial-credit score instead of the binary correct flag, and add a component-extraction step (list key-idea components present/missing) before scoring. LitNav already emits score_0_5 and has answer_key + evidence in-prompt — the signal is being thrown away.
citations: Grading Scale Impact on LLM-as-a-Judge, arXiv:2601.03444 (2026)

adversarial.reason: Citations check out and are on-point (AutoSCORE arXiv:2509.21910 = component-extraction-then-score improves QWK; arXiv:2601.03444 = 0-5 scale maximizes human-LLM alignment; arXiv:2601.08843 = rubric-conditioned grading), so the literature is sound. But the causal claim does not connect to the named metric. grading_acc is defined in litnav/eval/golden_llm.py:10-18 as bool(grade_fn(...)) == c["correct"] — a BINARY classifier accuracy. The grade_fn it uses is _live_grade in litnav/eval/run.py:24-39, which requests only {"correct": true/false} and returns a bool; score_0_5 is never in that path. Lever (a), driving kp_bump from score_0_5, feeds MASTERY (state.py:144), which is invisible to grading_acc — it would move avg_mastery_delta/mastered_rate, not grading_acc. Lever (b), component-extraction, could move the binary call, but only if added to _live_grade; the proposal frames it in the production grade_kp node, which the metric never exercises. Worse, the golden set data/eval/golden/grading.json is 16 cleanly-binary cases with ZERO partial-credit answers, so there is no partial-credit signal for the metric to recover — the 'signal being thrown away' premise is true of the node but untestable on the metric. Also a convention conflict: CLAUDE.md mandates mastery/routing be rule-computed, not LLM-emitted; feeding an LLM score_0_5 straight into kp_bump partially violates that. score_0_5 is indeed currently dead in grade_kp.py (line 100 reads only 'correct'), so the production critique is fair. Verdict revise, not drop: the underlying idea (component extraction before the correctness call) is legitimate and evidence-backed, but to move grading_acc the rec must be re-specified to (1) wire component-extraction into _live_grade, and (2) rebuild the golden set with partial-credit cases — otherwise it cannot move the metric it claims. As written it is mistargeted/cargo-cult relative to grading_acc; score is overstated at 7.5.

backlog.hypothesis: Fix venue/framing for submission: RouteLLM = ICLR 2025 (not 'ACL 2025'); ReAct = ICLR 2023 (2022 preprint); soften 'specialised KT always beats LLMs' to 'comparable-or-better accuracy at 600-12000x lower cost/latency'; add the SPECTER->text-embedding-3-small note to the storyboard table.

adversarial.reason: All three citations verified against arXiv and accurate. RouteLLM (arXiv:2406.18665) is ICLR 2025 ("Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2025" in the PDF header), NOT "ACL 2025" as the storyboard states — a real, trivially-reviewer-checkable error. ReAct (arXiv:2210.03629) is ICLR 2023 (Oct 2022 preprint) — the doc mislabels the venue. The specialised-KT paper (arXiv:2603.02830, Bhattacharyya et al., 3 Mar 2026) really is titled "Faster, Cheaper, More Accurate: Specialised KT Models Outperform LLMs" and the proposed reframing ("comparable-or-better accuracy at 600-12000x lower cost/latency") matches its abstract verbatim ("600-12,000 times less," "sub-second vs. minutes"); softening "always beats" to the cost/latency framing is the safer, stronger claim. Causal path to objective_quality (factual defensibility of the ICCSE submission) is direct and sound, not cargo-cult: fixing verifiable venue errors removes exactly the cheap mistakes that erode reviewer trust; effort S / risk low is accurate. Two reservations that argue for a possible revise rather than refute: (1) the SPECTER->text-embedding-3-small note is already largely present in both the storyboard (line 16) and methods (line 27) docs, so that sub-item is mostly redundant; (2) R8 ignores a MORE serious claim-vs-implementation gap the same docs carry — storyboard/methods assert BKT (Corbett & Anderson 1995) and Rasch/IRT mastery, but the real implementation is a hand-tuned bump rule plus a linear confidence=0.3*correct_obs gate, not a fitted BKT/Rasch model. That overclaim is exactly what a reviewer citing the KT paper would scrutinize, and fixing venues while leaving it would be inconsistent. Net: the evidence is solid and the corrections are correct and worth doing, so keep; the score of 7 is fair-to-slightly-generous, and the rec would be stronger if its scope were widened to also soften the BKT/IRT mastery claim to match the actual rule-based implementation.

foundations.note: Verified in code (feat/open-world-digest:litnav/state.py): the PRIMARY keypoint path uses kp_bump (hardcoded gains recall .25/comprehension .40/application .55, wrong-answer -.20) and kp_confidence = round(min(1, 0.30*correct_obs)) — a hand-tuned bump plus a linear correct-observation counter, NOT a Bayesian posterior. A real bkt_update() exists (slip .10/guess .20/transit .30) but the constants are textbook-fixed, never EM-fitted, and it is OFF the keypoint path. No per-skill params, no fit. The spec §11/§12 honestly says 'BKT-lite' and 'mastery is an estimate', but the per-step tables (methods ④d, storyboard ⑥) assert plain 'BKT — Corbett & Anderson 1995' with no -lite caveat. Overstatement of a heuristic in the reviewer-facing tables.
foundations.citation: Corbett, A. T., & Anderson, J. R. (1995). Knowledge Tracing. User Modeling and User-Adapted Interaction 4(4):253-278

foundations.note: irt_b is set by a one-shot weaker-LLM student simulator (+1/-1/0); irt_theta is a schema column that is never written or read (no online ability estimation). Methods ④c/④d cite 'Rasch/1PL (catsim/girth)' but no Rasch theta is fitted. The deliberately-WEAKER-simulator design choice for difficulty IS current best practice and correctly applied — 'Take Out Your Calculators' (arXiv:2601.09953) reports r up to .82 with weaker models beating stronger — so credit the simulator, flag the unfitted theta.

foundations.method: RefD — prerequisite relation measurement (Liang et al., EMNLP 2015)
foundations.note: Verified (litnav/digest/refd.py + OPEN-WORLD-STATUS): the signal is chunk-term co-occurrence / reference-distance asymmetry over the corpus's OWN chunks, NOT Liang 2015's Wikipedia link-reference-graph RefD with relatedness weights. The spec hedges 'RefD-style'; the storyboard/methods tables cite 'RefD — Liang et al. EMNLP 2015' with no -style qualifier. ACE (JEDM 2024) is the precise published precedent for the actual structural-score + LLM-judge-and-explain pattern and should be the direct citation. The two-signal design itself is legitimate and worked live (RefD recovered in_context_learning→agentic_reasoning that the judge rejected).
foundations.citation: Liang, Wu, Huang & Giles (2015), EMNLP, ACL D15-1193; Aytekin & Saygın, ACE, JEDM 16(2) 2024

foundations.note: Methods table tags concept/keypoint extraction as 'GraphRAG-style'. GraphRAG (arXiv:2404.16130) is query-focused SUMMARIZATION over an entity graph — not concept-graph-for-teaching or prerequisite induction. The actual operation is concept/keyphrase extraction + prereq edges; the label is a buzzword mismatch a reviewer will catch. Cite concept-map / prereq-relation-mining literature (e.g. the ACM Comput. Surv. 2025 prereq survey) instead.
foundations.citation: Edge et al. (2024), arXiv:2404.16130; Zhang et al., ACM Comput. Surv. 57(11) 2025

foundations.note: Cited in the find-sources rerank but substituted by text-embedding-3-small cosine. This substitution is DISCLOSED in both the spec and methods table ('SPECTER → substituted by text-embedding-3-small cosine'). Correctly handled — keep the disclosure; add it to the storyboard table for consistency.
Used as the keyword prefilter before embedding rerank in rank.py — a standard, correctly-applied retrieval cascade. No issue.
foundations.citation: Robertson & Zaragoza, The Probabilistic Relevance Framework: BM25 and Beyond

foundations.note: Cost spine uses a cheap→frontier cascade with confidence-gated escalation. LitNavigator's deviation (route on pedagogical-error cost near the mastery band, not token cost) is a defensible, well-argued improvement over the FrugalGPT default, not a misuse.

Correctly applied as routing inspiration. VENUE ERROR for submission: methods table says 'RouteLLM (ACL 2025)'; it is ICLR 2025 (arXiv:2406.18665). Fix the tag. Note the escalation trigger is currently a hand-set threshold (CONF_MIN=0.6 + band), not the learned router RouteLLM advocates — an honest 'inspired-by'.

Verified (litnav/assess/quizgen.py:flaw_gate): the gate is STRUCTURAL-ONLY — empty stem, <2 distinct distractors, distractor==answer (≈3 checks). SAQUET screens ~19 item-writing flaws (cueing, implausible/heterogeneous distractors, all-of-the-above, negative stems, grammatical clues). Docstring itself says 'SAQUET-style structural flaw check', but the methods table cites it as the SAQUET flaw gate without that scoping. Aspirational label for a thinner implementation.

foundations.method: Overgenerate-and-rank distractors (Scarlatos et al., NAACL 2024 BEA)
foundations.note: LITERAL claim-vs-implementation gap, verified in quizgen.make_distractors: the docstring says 'overgenerate ~6 candidates, then... Returns the first n' — there is NO rank step; it overgenerates 6, drops the answer, dedups, and takes the FIRST n. Scarlatos 2024 is explicit that ranking-by-student-plausibility is the step that makes the method work. The 'and-rank' is currently false.
foundations.citation: Scarlatos et al. (2024), NAACL 2024 BEA Workshop

foundations.note: spacing.py is FSRS-lite (interval = 1/(1-mastery), x2 fast-forward at ≥0.95) with no fitted DSR stability/lapse state. Worse, review_queue items are enqueued but never DELIVERED in the storyboard — the retrieval-practice benefit is dormant. Methods ④e cites 'FSRS'; the implementation is a heuristic spacer with the probe loop unwired.
foundations.citation: Open Spaced Repetition / FSRS DSR model (Ye et al. 2022-2023)

foundations.note: The 'never LLM self-judge' design principle is correctly adopted and well-grounded. But the framing overstates the source: arXiv:2603.02830 actually shows LLM-KT narrowly TOP (72.8% vs SAKT 72.7%); the durable, defensible claim is comparable-or-better accuracy at 600-12000x lower cost/latency, not universal accuracy dominance. Soften to avoid a reviewer rebuttal that undercuts an otherwise-correct principle.
foundations.citation: Faster, Cheaper, More Accurate: Specialised KT Models Outperform LLMs, arXiv:2603.02830

foundations.method: ReAct (Yao et al.) + Plan-and-Solve (Wang et al. 2023) orchestration
foundations.note: The outer-loop TOPOLOGY is described as ReAct+Plan-and-Solve, but the plan is implicit branching — no emitted/logged plan artifact, and reteach has no Reflexion-style error memory (reteach_kp flips strategy by a MAX_RETEACH counter). VENUE: methods cites 'ReAct — Yao et al. 2022'; it is arXiv:2210.03629 (2022 preprint) but ICLR 2023 — use 'Yao et al., ICLR 2023 (arXiv 2022)'. The design philosophy aligns with the evidence; implementation depth is the gap.
foundations.citation: Yao et al., ReAct, arXiv:2210.03629 (ICLR 2023); Wang et al., Plan-and-Solve, ACL 2023

foundations.method: Worked-example effect & expertise reversal (Sweller/Kalyuga; Barbieri et al. 2023 meta-analysis)
foundations.note: 'worked_example' is a STATIC label applied at mastery<0.35 with a 'concise' label for experts — no fading ladder (full example → completion problem → full problem). The durable worked-example benefit comes from FADING, and expertise reversal means an unfaded example HURTS advanced learners. The meta-analysis citation is real and the principle correctly named, but the fading mechanism the literature requires is absent.
foundations.citation: Barbieri et al. (2023), Educational Psychology Review 35:11; Sweller/Kalyuga cognitive load

foundations.note: Interleaving is effectively ABSENT: the route is strictly prereq-ordered/blocked. concept_edges.similarity already exists and gives a principled trigger (interleaving helps g≈0.42 ONLY for confusable material — 'similarity matters'), so the lever is named in the data model but unused.

foundations.method: Testing/retrieval-practice effect (Roediger & Karpicke 2006; Yang et al. 2021)
foundations.note: The testing effect is used for GRADING (assessment) but not for memory STRENGTHENING: review_queue + retention_log tables are built but no spaced retrieval PROBE is ever delivered before new teaching. Retrieval practice is durable (g≈0.50; transfer d≈0.40) — the data layer exists but the lever is dormant. Correctly cited, not yet operationalized as a strengthening mechanism.
foundations.citation: Roediger & Karpicke (2006), Psychological Science 17(3):249-255; Yang et al. (2021), Psychological Bulletin 147(4):399-435

foundations.note: Applied in teach_kp and the artifact renderers (anti-verbatim Cornell notes, coherent slides). Reasonable application of multimedia/coherence principles; no overstatement found.

foundations.method: Bloom-leveled question generation (Scaria et al., AIED 2024)
foundations.note: assess_next passes a one-line per-level spec but no Bloom DEFINITION and no exemplars. Scaria 2024 finds adequate info + exemplars are what enable the higher rungs; comprehension/application items are therefore the weakest link. Correctly cited, under-applied at the prompt level.

foundations.method: Mastery threshold / mastery learning (EDM 2025; Winget & Persky 2022)
foundations.note: KP_MASTERY_THRESHOLD=0.75 (advance) is lenient vs the mastery-learning literature (EDM 2025: 0.95 standard, 0.98 for transfer). Advancing at 0.75 risks under-mastery; the threshold is neither surfaced nor justified against a retention probe. The honest g≈0.27 ITS-gain framing the spec uses elsewhere IS correctly applied.
foundations.citation: Zhang et al., How Much Mastery is Enough Mastery, EDM 2025; Winget & Persky (2022), AJPE 86(10):8906

gaps: BKT/Rasch overstatement (load-bearing): the primary keypoint mastery path is a hand-tuned kp_bump + linear kp_confidence=0.3*correct_obs, with no fitted slip/guess/transit and no Rasch theta (irt_theta is a schema column never read/written); the spec §11/§12 honestly says 'BKT-lite/estimate' but the reviewer-facing per-step tables (methods ④d, storyboard ⑥) assert plain 'BKT — Corbett & Anderson 1995'. This is the single biggest claim-vs-implementation gap.

Two literal docstring-vs-code gaps a reviewer will catch by reading one function: make_distractors claims 'overgenerate-and-rank' but takes the first-n (no rank step), and flaw_gate is called 'SAQUET-style' but screens ~3 structural flaws vs SAQUET's ~19 item-writing flaws.

RefD mis-attribution and GraphRAG mislabel: refd.py is corpus co-occurrence/reference-distance asymmetry, not Liang 2015's Wikipedia link-graph RefD; concept extraction is tagged 'GraphRAG-style' though GraphRAG is query-focused summarization. ACE (JEDM 2024) is the correct precedent for the actual two-signal pattern.

No global-consistency / cycle-breaking pass over prereq edges (GKROM/DGCPL show this is the accuracy frontier); LitNavigator scores edges only locally/pairwise, and single-source digest produces 0 surviving prereq edges.

Learning-science levers are named but dormant: spaced retrieval probes are enqueued (review_queue/retention_log) but never delivered before teaching; worked examples are a static label with no fading ladder; interleaving is unused despite concept_edges.similarity providing a principled trigger. Testing effect is used for grading, not for strengthening.

Orchestration depth: ReAct+Plan-and-Solve is topology-only (implicit branching, no logged plan artifact) and reteach has no Reflexion-style error memory (counter-driven strategy flip). Escalation objective (pedagogical-error cost near the mastery band) is correct but the trigger is hand-set, not calibrated from logged data.

Grading throws away signal: grade_kp emits score_0_5 + confidence but drives mastery off the BINARY correct flag; no component-extraction step and no self-consistency (k-sample) near the threshold, despite the 0-5 scale being where human-LLM alignment peaks.

Open-web live edge accuracy is unvalidated (spec risk A); LitNavigator cannot claim benchmark-grade accuracy and should report a live self-measured edge-accuracy number instead of closed-corpus numbers.

Citation/venue errors for ICCSE submission: RouteLLM is ICLR 2025 (methods says 'ACL 2025'); ReAct is ICLR 2023 / 2022 preprint; the 'specialised KT always beats LLM' framing overstates arXiv:2603.02830 (which shows LLM-KT narrowly top).
